title: Business Service Availability (Composite SLA)
app: Splunk ITSI, or custom lookups + summary indexing
spl:
index=monitoring sourcetype IN (server_health, app_health, db_health) earliest=-24h
| eval up=if(status="ok" OR status="up",1,0)
| stats latest(up) as up by component_name
| lookup business_service_map.csv component_name OUTPUT business_service, weight, is_essential
| eval w=coalesce(weight,1)
| stats max(eval(if((is_essential=1 OR lower(is_essential)="true") AND up=0,1,0))) as essential_down
       sum(eval(w*up)) as weighted_up
       sum(w) as total_weight by business_service
| eval composite_sla=if(essential_down>0, 0, round(100*weighted_up/total_weight,2))
| where composite_sla < 99.9 OR essential_down>0
| table business_service, composite_sla, essential_down
description: Rolls up component availability into a single business-service SLA percentage (weighted or “all essential up”) for customer-facing reporting — beyond host-level heatmaps.
value: Rolls up component availability into a single business-service SLA percentage (weighted or “all essential up”) for customer-facing reporting — beyond host-level heatmaps.
implementation: Define `business_service_map.csv` with components, optional weights, and `is_essential` (any essential down forces SLA=0 or “breach”). Ingest normalized availability per component every 5 minutes; backfill from summary index for monthly SLA. Align with contract SLAs (e.g., 99.9% monthly). ITSI can replace this with service KPIs and composite service health.
visualization: Single value (composite SLA % per service), Bar chart (SLA vs target), Table (breaching services), ITSI Service Analyzer (if licensed).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI, or custom lookups + summary indexing.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=monitoring` normalized health events, `business_service_map.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Define `business_service_map.csv` with components, optional weights, and `is_essential` (any essential down forces SLA=0 or “breach”). Ingest normalized availability per component every 5 minutes; backfill from summary index for monthly SLA. Align with contract SLAs (e.g., 99.9% monthly). ITSI can replace this with service KPIs and composite service health.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=monitoring sourcetype IN (server_health, app_health, db_health) earliest=-24h
| eval up=if(status="ok" OR status="up",1,0)
| stats latest(up) as up by component_name
| lookup business_service_map.csv component_name OUTPUT business_service, weight, is_essential
| eval w=coalesce(weight,1)
| stats max(eval(if((is_essential=1 OR lower(is_essential)="true") AND up=0,1,0))) as essential_down
       sum(eval(w*up)) as weighted_up
       sum(w) as total_weight by business_service
| eval composite_sla=if(essential_down>0, 0, round(100*weighted_up/total_weight,2))
| where composite_sla < 99.9 OR essential_down>0
| table business_service, composite_sla, essential_down
```

Understanding this SPL

**Business Service Availability (Composite SLA)** — Rolls up component availability into a single business-service SLA percentage (weighted or “all essential up”) for customer-facing reporting — beyond host-level heatmaps.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=monitoring` normalized health events, `business_service_map.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI, or custom lookups + summary indexing. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: monitoring.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=monitoring, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **up** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by component_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **w** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by business_service** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **composite_sla** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where composite_sla < 99.9 OR essential_down>0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Business Service Availability (Composite SLA)**): table business_service, composite_sla, essential_down


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (composite SLA % per service), Bar chart (SLA vs target), Table (breaching services), ITSI Service Analyzer (if licensed).
grandmaExplanation: Rolls up component availability into a single business-service service promises percentage (weighted or “all essential up”) for customer-facing reporting — beyond host-level heatmaps — so tickets, changes, and incidents are on track and nothing is silently missed.
